one_line_takeaway: A lightweight, LLM-agnostic outer layer that uses partially blind signatures to make paid AIaaS requests unlinkable to the paying user for both subscription and API billing models, adding near-zero latency and only ~0.7-1.1 KB of payload per request while deliberately NOT hiding query content.
key_finding: Adding anonymity via partially blind signatures costs almost nothing in latency and only a small, bounded payload increase. Time overhead vs. the no-signature baseline is at most a couple of seconds and often statistically indistinguishable from network noise (Q1 API mode: mean +1.2s, p=0.0007 i.e. detectable but negligible; Q2: not significant, API p=0.36, Sub p=0.3264). The real cost is payload: request size roughly doubles-to-triples (Q1: 652 B normal -> 1321 B API -> 1768 B subscription) because the request now carries the user public key and blinded digest, and subscription-mode responses grow by a near-constant ~361 B because they must return a freshly issued signature (API-mode responses are unchanged, e.g. Q1 169 B in both normal and API). Overhead does not grow superlinearly with concurrency (5 vs 10 users) and is model-agnostic: switching the LLM changes absolute numbers but not the inter-mode differences, which stem solely from the signature operations. Batch generation of 10 signatures took 0.78 s (request 3676 B, response 7019 B).
core_contribution: Proposes an application-layer framework that gives users anonymity (request-to-user unlinkability) when consuming online LLM 'AI as a Service' (AIaaS), without modifying the underlying LLM. The mechanism is a partially blind signature (PBS): the user blinds a locally generated 'ticket', the provider signs it, and the user later submits the unblinded ticket+signature alongside a request in an anonymous session, so the server can validate entitlement without linking the request to a paying identity. The paper's genuine novelty is not the PBS primitive itself (an existing RSA-based scheme from cited work [35]) but two billing-compatible strategies that make anonymity coexist with real business models: (1) a subscription mode where the signature carries a STANDARDIZED deadline (e.g. 1st of the month) so many users share the same public metadata, and the provider re-issues one fresh signature per completed query (no need to store a used-signature list); and (2) an API mode that re-defines charging from per-token to per-request with a fixed max-tokens-per-request cap, plus a maintained used-signature list, so quota can be enforced anonymously. Explicitly framed as a black-box outer layer requiring no changes to LLM internals.
threat_model: Three adversary scenarios plus side channels. (1) Man-in-the-middle between user and server aiming to read/modify traffic or impersonate either party. (2) A corrupted/malicious user aiming to obtain service beyond what was paid (forge or reuse signatures). (3) A corrupted/honest-but-curious server aiming to link user identities to their requests, or to link requests to each other. Additionally considers side-channel/timing correlation and IP-based tracking. Notably the model does NOT try to hide request CONTENT from the server: unlike the HE/MPC/DP lines it contrasts against, the server sees plaintext queries; the security goal is unlinkability of who-asked-what, with the argument that since no login is required, correlated query threads still cannot be tied to an identity. IP-layer tracking is pushed out of scope by recommending Tor. Anonymity provably reduces to the unlinkability (partial blindness) of the PBS scheme; interest-protection reduces to PBS unforgeability; secure communication is provided by PKI/TLS.
limitations: (1) Confidentiality of query CONTENT is out of scope entirely: the server processes plaintext prompts, so sensitive data inside a prompt is still exposed to the provider; the framework only unlinks requests from identities. This is a fundamentally weaker guarantee than the HE/MPC/DP schemes it cites, and the two are not head-to-head comparable. (2) Anonymity is an anonymity-set argument: it holds only if many users genuinely share the same standardized deadline / fixed charging plan; small or idiosyncratic user populations weaken it, and this is asserted rather than empirically measured. (3) Side-channel defense against timing/IP tracking is partly delegated to Tor (external, not evaluated). (4) Evaluation is small-scale: only five short factual questions, n=10 repetitions, one server/client pair, up to 10 concurrent users. (5) No experiment actually runs the cited HE/MPC/DP baselines on the same hardware; those numbers (e.g. BumbleBee, Iron) come from other papers, so cross-scheme claims rest on literature values, not this paper's measurements. (6) Reported time deviations (normal sometimes slower than API) are attributed to HTTPS/network variance, which muddies the precision of the overhead claims.

datasets_used: No training/benchmark dataset; models are pretrained and unmodified
DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-14B (primary evaluation model, from Hugging Face)
Custom probe set of 5 fixed factual questions Q1-Q5 (e.g. tallest mountain, Planck constant), each repeated n=10; plus 5 'long questions' LQ1-LQ5 for concurrency tests
quantitative_results: Q1 end-to-end time: normal 37.7s, API 36.5s, subscription 37.3s (overhead within noise)
Q1 request size: normal 652 B -> API 1321.4 B -> subscription 1767.8 B
Q1 response size: normal 169 B, API 169 B (unchanged), subscription 530 B
API-mode time overhead vs baseline for Q1: mean +1.2s, statistically significant (p=0.0007) but practically negligible
Q2 time overhead: not statistically significant (API p=0.3600, subscription p=0.3264)
Subscription-mode response payload overhead: near-constant +361 B across Q1-Q5 (the returned fresh signature); API-mode response overhead 0 B
Batch generation of 10 signatures in one request: 0.78 s, request 3676 B, response 7019 B
Concurrency (DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-14B) elapsed time: ~89-92s at 5 users, ~132-151s at 10 users across api/sub/normal modes (overhead stable, no superlinear growth)
Crypto parameters: RSA-based PBS with 2048-bit modulus; hash functions HM and HMD derived from SHA-384
Network testbed (Table II): ping RTT avg ~23-24 ms (min 22, max ~80-82 ms); TCP download bandwidth highly variable (5.88-73.6 Mbit/s), motivating HTTPS-variance explanation for time anomalies
baselines_compared: PAPER'S OWN experimental baseline: 'normal' mode = the same LLM service WITHOUT partially blind signatures (only internal comparison actually run)

CITED-ONLY (Related Work, NOT re-run by the authors): HE schemes Iron [7], BumbleBee [8], THE-X [6]; MPC schemes Privformer [20]/[10], East [9]; secret-sharing Sigma [11]; differential-privacy DP-Forward [22] and perturbation [21] -- these hide content, a different goal, and their numbers are quoted from their own papers
